title: Punk Rocker Egrets Steal the Show at Smithsonian Photo Contest
summary: Imagine three baby birds with spiky feathers that look exactly like punk rocker mohawks, squabbling and pecking at each other while their giant parent looms in the background. That's the grand-prize-winning image of the 23rd annual Smithsonian Magazine Photo Contest. Photographer Jacqueline Burke, a 64-year-old veterinarian from Perkiomenville, Pennsylvania, titled her entry "The Punk Rockers." She captured the moment at a rookery (a breeding ground for birds) under a ramp at the Ocean City welcome center in New Jersey. "Their little necks and heads were writhing," Burke says. "One would peck. The other would peck back." The chicks were no larger than golf balls when curled up, and their parent's long legs and imposing feet create a dramatic scale contrast. "The photographer framed it beautifully," says Smithsonian creative director Maria Keehan. The contest received more than 17,000 submissions from 108 countries. Other winners include a barefoot devotee expressing herself during the Hindu festival of Shivaratri, captured by photographer Nalimela in Telangana, India. "You don't always need a visible face. What truly matters is whether you can feel the essence of the scene," Nalimela says. Another winner shows 14-year-old DelShay Henio during a traditional Navajo Kinaaldá ceremony, a four-day ritual welcoming her to womanhood, as she leads her horse Hazel to graze. "It's a quiet, soft portrait of a young woman looking toward her future," says photographer Cara Carpenter. A conservation photographer captured a rare chameleon in a garden, and an aerial photo shows the circular Oppo Chang An R&D Center in China. Burke, who traded binoculars for a camera in 2014, hopes her image inspires conservation: "We need to save species like this and save moments like this."
